ELCODE: Unique NatureServe Element Code for the taxon. As long as the taxonomic concept is unchanged, this code remains the same even if the scientific name changes.
GS Name: Species' scientific name used in 2010 National General Status ranking effort. Note that only binomials (no subspecies or varieties) were given General Status ranks, so subspecies and variety names use AC  CDC taxonomy except where an appropriate trinomial is not listed in Flora of North America for a species with different AC CDC and General Status scientific names. For example, no varieties of Rumex brittanica (=Rumex orbiculatus by AC CDC taxonomy] are recognized Flora of North America, so both of the AC CDC taxa R. orbiculatus and R. orbiculatus var. borealis are called R. brittanica under GS taxonomy.
GS English Name: English name used in 2010 National General Status ranking effort. Species names for subspecies and varieties are just duplicates of the names for the binomial (Genus + species) combination. Hybrids taxa were not considered under the General Status process and have no English names listed.
AC CDC Name: Species' scientific name used in the AC CDC database, mostly in agreement with Kartesz (1999)
AC CDC English Name: English name used in the AC CDC database. Names are from NatureServe headquarters in Virginia.
GS French Name: French name used in 2010 National General Status ranking effort. Species names for subspecies and varieties are just duplicates of the names for the binomial (Genus + species) combination. Hybrids taxa were not considered under the General Status process and have no English names listed.
AC CDC French Name: French name used in the AC CDC database, most of which are from Flore Laurentienne.
AC CDC Binomial Only: Genus + species combination (excluding subspecies or variety if present) used in the AC CDC database.
AC CDC ssp/var. only: Subspecies or variety only for subspecific taxa (excluding Genus + species combination), as used in the AC CDC database.
Native Hybrid Parental Taxa: Parental taxa of native hybrid taxa. Exotic hybrids do not have parental taxa named because they have been introduced as hybrids rather than being derived naturally in the field.
N-rank: NatureServe Canada national status rank for Canada.
S-rank: AC CDC subnational rank for Nova Scotia, updated to April 2011.
NGS Rank: Canadian National General Status rank for 2010.
GS Rank: Nova Scotia National General Status rank for 2010.
rare: exotic species, rare in Nova Scotia
uncommon: exotic species, uncommon in Nova Scotia
fairly common: exotic species, fairly common in Nova Scotia
locally common: exotic species, locally common in one or a few regions in Nova Scotia but otherwise rare
widespread: exotic species, widespread in Nova Scotia
various: overall phytogeographic affinity, as recorded in Meades et al. 2000. Annotated Checklist of the Vascular Plants of Newfoundland and Labrador.
x: occurring in peatlands but not primarily associated with richer fen communities; these are generally species of open conditions, though many will also occur under forest canopy in forested peatland
y: characteristic of southern Nova Scotia lakeshores, including associated open wetlands but lacking floristic affinity to Atlantic Coastal Plain
xa: aquatic species characteristic of southern Nova Scotia lakes AND with floristic affinity to Atlantic Coastal Plain
xb: Species of richer deciduous forests in Nova Scotia with limited range extension south of Nova Scotia into northern USA as mapped in BONAP's North American Plant Atlas online.
ya: aquatic species characteristic of southern Nova Scotia lakes, including associated open wetlands but lacking floristic affinity to Atlantic Coastal Plain
w: species assigned OBL or FACW wetland indicator status for NS
obl: Obligate Wetland; occurs almost always (estimated probability 99%) under natural conditions in wetlands.
facw: Facultative Wetland; usually occurs in wetlands (estimated probability 67%-99%), but occasionally found in non-wetlands.
fac: Facultative; equally likely to occur in wetlands or non-wetlands (estimated probability 34%-66%).
facu: Facultative Upland; occurs in non-wetlands (estimated probability 67%-99%), but occasionally found on wetlands (estimated probability 1%-33%).
upl: Obligate Upland ; occurs almost always (estimated probability 99%) under natural conditions in non-wetlands.
NI: No indicator; insufficient information was available to determine an indicator status.
f: occurring in peatlands primarily in association with richer fen communities
fo: occurs primarily under forest canopy, but also in open
efo: occurs roughly equally under forest canopy or in open
of: occurs primarily  in open, but also under forest canopy,
c1: fairly strong indicator of calcareous conditions
c2: slight or possible indicator of calcareous conditions; often strong or fairly strong indicator when not in coastal area
ic: fairly indifferent to soil pH, but perhaps leaning to the more calcareous
a-: slight or possible indicator of acidic conditions
b: occurring in brackish conditions but typically excluded from full salinity sites
forest: associated with seeps and springs under forest cover (sometimes in quite small patches within uplands)
s: submergent aquatic; plants typically fully underwater except for floating leaves and/or floating or emergent inflorescences
e: emergent aquatic; plants typically or frequently with bases fully submerged in freshwater through most or all of growing season, but with leaves and inflorescences above water.  Saltmarsh plants are excluded from this category since they are more or less emergent by definition.
m: plants strongly associated with floodplain terrace meadows along rivers or streams, sometimes including sites that are wet for only a short period annually
mf: plants strongly associated with floodplain terrace meadows and forests along rivers or streams, sometimes including sites that are wet for only a short period annually
ranks as above under NS Wetland Indicato: definitions as above, but with ranks applying to the United States Fish and Wildlife Service's Northeast Region
Match status: proposed Nova Scotia and USFWS Northeast Region wetland indicator status ranks match exactly, independent of "+" or "-" suffixes
no match: proposed Nova Scotia and USFWS Northeast Region wetland indicator status ranks do not match exactly
no US rank: no wetland indicator status ranks is available for USFWS Northeast Region
